prompt: Why is electricity considered an 'invisible danger'?

choices.text: It only flows at night when you can't see it
You cannot see, smell, or hear it in a wire
It moves too fast for the human eye
It only becomes dangerous after a power surge

hint: Think about your senses — can any of them detect current flowing through a wire?

solution.finalAnswer: You cannot see, smell, or hear it in a wire.

steps.title: Recognise the core danger
steps.body: Unlike fire or a sharp blade, electricity gives no sensory warning. A live wire looks identical to a dead one.

steps.title: Understand the implication
steps.body: Because you cannot detect it with your senses, you must always assume a wire could be live and test before touching.

solution.whyItWorks: Safety awareness starts with understanding that electricity provides no visible or audible cue — you must rely on tools and procedures to stay safe.

commonMistakes: Assuming a wire is safe because it 'looks fine' — appearance tells you nothing about whether current is flowing.

prompt: Which of the following is a correct reason to wear rubber-soled shoes when working near electrical equipment?

choices.text: Rubber keeps your feet warm near high-voltage equipment
Rubber is an insulator and reduces the chance of current flowing through your body to ground
Rubber shoes are required by fashion guidelines on job sites
Rubber prevents static electricity from building up

hint: Think about what materials block the flow of electricity.

solution.finalAnswer: Rubber is an insulator and reduces the chance of current flowing through your body to ground.

steps.title: Recall what insulators do
steps.body: Insulators resist the flow of electric current. Rubber is one of the best common insulators.

steps.title: Apply to the body path
steps.body: If you accidentally touch a live conductor, current needs a path through your body to ground. Rubber soles break that path, greatly reducing the risk of shock.

solution.whyItWorks: Personal protective equipment (PPE) like insulated footwear creates a barrier in the electrical path, which is a fundamental principle of electrical safety.

commonMistakes: Thinking any shoe is fine — only insulating materials like rubber provide meaningful protection against electrical current.

prompt: When should you call a licensed electrician instead of doing the work yourself?

choices.text: Only when the job costs more than $500
Whenever the work involves your home's main panel, new circuits, or anything you're unsure about
Never — basic online research is enough for any electrical job

hint: Think about situations where a mistake could cause serious harm or violate building codes.

solution.finalAnswer: Whenever the work involves your home's main panel, new circuits, or anything you're unsure about.

steps.title: Identify high-risk work
steps.body: Work on the main breaker panel, adding new circuits, and anything involving permits or inspections should always be done by a licensed professional.

steps.title: Apply the uncertainty rule
steps.body: If you are unsure about any part of an electrical task — the wiring, the codes, the safety steps — that uncertainty itself is the signal to call a professional.

solution.whyItWorks: Licensed electricians have training, insurance, and knowledge of local codes. The cost of hiring one is always less than the cost of a serious accident or a house fire.

commonMistakes: Thinking a quick YouTube video is sufficient for complex electrical work — building codes and safety procedures vary by location and require professional judgement.
